title: The mathematics of forgiveness
summary: A debt of money can be cancelled with a stroke of a pen. A debt of harm cannot — and yet we keep trying to do the same arithmetic on both. The essay asks what forgiveness actually does.
body:
There is a strange asymmetry in how we treat debts. A debt of money can usually be cancelled with a stroke of a pen. The lender writes 'forgiven', the borrower is free, and the world moves on. A debt of harm, however — a betrayal, a cruelty, a broken promise — cannot be cancelled the same way. Even when the wronged party says, sincerely, 'I forgive you,' something stays.

Why is this? The two debts seem, on the surface, comparable. Both are owed by one person to another. Both can be expressed, in a sense, as a quantity: a number of dong, a number of hours of pain. Both can in principle be repaid, or written off. And yet anyone who has tried to forgive a real injury knows that the procedure is not as clean as it is with money.

One answer is that money is fungible — one note is the same as another — whereas harm is not. The specific harm I did you cannot be undone by an equivalent payment from me, because the original event has already happened. You can be compensated, perhaps, but the event remains. Forgiveness, then, is not the cancellation of the debt; it is a decision to stop charging interest on it.

This sheds light on a common error. People sometimes think that forgiveness means pretending the injury did not occur, or feeling no further pain about it. By this standard, they conclude that they have 'failed' to forgive, and judge themselves harshly. But this is the wrong test. The remembered injury is part of the truth. Forgiveness asks only that you stop using the injury as a current weapon — against the other, or against yourself.

There is a further complication. Some injuries are owed not to the person but to the relationship. A friend who lied to you owes a debt to you; but they also owe a debt to the friendship itself, which has been weakened. You can forgive them personally, and yet the friendship may not return to its old shape. This is not a failure of forgiveness. It is the recognition that some debts have more than one creditor.

There is no formula for any of this. The mathematics of forgiveness, if we can call it that, is the mathematics of subtraction, not of erasure. Something is taken off the account. Something else, irreducibly, stays. To pretend otherwise is to misunderstand what forgiveness is for. It is not a way of unhappening the past. It is a way of letting the future arrive.

annotations.meaningVi: có thể thay thế lẫn nhau (về giá trị) — tờ tiền này bằng tờ tiền kia
vũ khí (ở đây dùng theo nghĩa ẩn dụ)
sự xóa bỏ, sự xóa sạch

grammarNotes.title: Concession with 'and yet' / 'even when'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Trong văn nghị luận, 'and yet' và 'even when' là hai cách thông dụng để nhường một điều (chấp nhận một sự thật quen thuộc) trước khi đưa ra ý ngược lại. Trong bài: 'Even when the wronged party says, sincerely, "I forgive you," something stays.' Cấu trúc này thừa nhận điều người đọc có thể đã tin, rồi lập luận ngược lại một cách nhẹ nhàng. Cũng xem: 'And yet anyone who has tried to forgive a real injury knows that the procedure is not as clean…'
grammarNotes.bodyEn: In essay writing, 'and yet' and 'even when' are common ways to concede a point — admit it is true — before raising the counter-claim. From the essay: 'Even when the wronged party says, sincerely, "I forgive you," something stays.' The structure acknowledges what the reader may believe, then quietly disagrees. See also: 'And yet anyone who has tried to forgive a real injury knows that the procedure is not as clean…'

cloze.template:
There is a strange {{b1}} in how we treat debts. A debt of money can usually be {{b2}} with a stroke of a pen. The lender writes 'forgiven', the borrower is free, and the world moves on. A debt of harm, however — a {{b3}}, a cruelty, a broken promise — cannot be cancelled the same way. Even when the wronged party says, sincerely, 'I forgive you,' something stays.

Why is this? The two debts seem, on the surface, comparable. Both are owed by one person to another. Both can be expressed, in a sense, as a quantity: a number of dong, a number of hours of pain. Both can in principle be repaid, or written off. And yet anyone who has tried to forgive a real injury knows that the procedure is not as clean as it is with money.

One answer is that money is {{b4}} — one note is the same as another — whereas harm is not. The specific harm I did you cannot be undone by an equivalent payment from me, because the original event has already happened. You can be {{b5}}, perhaps, but the event remains. Forgiveness, then, is not the cancellation of the debt; it is a decision to stop charging {{b6}} on it.

This sheds light on a common error. People sometimes think that forgiveness means {{b7}} the injury did not occur, or feeling no further pain about it. By this standard, they conclude that they have 'failed' to forgive, and judge themselves {{b8}}. But this is the wrong test. The remembered injury is part of the truth. Forgiveness asks only that you stop using the injury as a current {{b9}} — against the other, or against yourself.

There is a further complication. Some injuries are owed not to the person but to the {{b10}}. A friend who lied to you owes a debt to you; but they also owe a debt to the friendship itself, which has been {{b11}}. You can forgive them personally, and yet the friendship may not return to its old shape. This is not a failure of forgiveness. It is the recognition that some debts have more than one {{b12}}.

There is no {{b13}} for any of this. The mathematics of forgiveness, if we can call it that, is the mathematics of {{b14}}, not of {{b15}}. Something is taken off the account. Something else, irreducibly, stays. To pretend otherwise is to misunderstand what forgiveness is for. It is not a way of unhappening the past. It is a way of letting the {{b16}} arrive.

blanks.explanation: An asymmetry — an imbalance between two things that seem comparable but aren't.
A debt of money can be cancelled (formally erased) with a stroke of a pen.
A betrayal — one of the writer's three examples of harm.
Money is fungible — one note is the same as another. Harm is not.
You can be compensated for harm — paid back in some way — but the event itself remains.
Forgiveness is 'a decision to stop charging interest' on the debt — the debt remains; the punishment for it does not grow.
Pretending the injury did not occur — the common error the writer wants to correct.
They judge themselves harshly — too severely — for failing a test that is itself wrong.
Stop using the injury as a current weapon — against the other or yourself.
Some injuries are owed not just to the person but to the relationship itself.
The friendship itself has been weakened — damaged in a way personal forgiveness cannot fully repair.
Some debts have more than one creditor — the person and the relationship both have a claim.
There is no formula — no fixed equation — for forgiveness.
The mathematics of subtraction — something is taken away from the account, not erased completely.
Not erasure — forgiveness does not pretend the event never happened.
It is 'a way of letting the future arrive' — the closing image and the central purpose of forgiveness.

translationVi:
Có một sự bất đối xứng kỳ lạ trong cách chúng ta đối xử với các món nợ. Một món nợ tiền thường có thể được xóa bằng một nét bút. Người cho vay viết 'tha', người vay được tự do, và thế giới đi tiếp. Một món nợ tổn thương, tuy nhiên — một sự phản bội, một sự tàn nhẫn, một lời hứa bị bẻ gãy — không thể được xóa cùng cách ấy. Ngay cả khi người bị làm tổn thương nói, chân thành, 'Tôi tha thứ cho bạn,' điều gì đó vẫn còn lại.

Vì sao vậy? Hai món nợ, nhìn bề mặt, có vẻ tương đương. Cả hai đều là nợ giữa hai người. Cả hai, theo một nghĩa nào đó, đều có thể biểu đạt bằng một đại lượng: một số đồng, một số giờ đau đớn. Cả hai về nguyên tắc đều có thể trả, hoặc xóa. Vậy mà ai từng cố gắng tha thứ cho một tổn thương thực sự đều biết rằng quy trình không sạch sẽ như với tiền.

Một câu trả lời là tiền thì có thể thay thế lẫn nhau — một tờ tiền này giống một tờ tiền khác — trong khi tổn thương thì không. Sự tổn thương cụ thể tôi đã gây ra cho bạn không thể được tháo gỡ bằng một khoản chi trả tương đương từ tôi, bởi vì sự kiện gốc đã xảy ra rồi. Bạn có thể được đền bù, có lẽ, nhưng sự kiện vẫn còn lại. Sự tha thứ, vì vậy, không phải là việc xóa nợ; mà là quyết định ngừng tính lãi trên món nợ ấy.

Điều này soi sáng một lỗi thường gặp. Có người nghĩ rằng tha thứ có nghĩa là vờ rằng tổn thương không xảy ra, hoặc không còn cảm thấy đau gì nữa. Theo tiêu chuẩn ấy, họ kết luận rằng họ đã 'thất bại' trong việc tha thứ, và phán xét bản thân khắt khe. Nhưng đó là tiêu chuẩn sai. Tổn thương được nhớ là một phần của sự thật. Tha thứ chỉ yêu cầu bạn ngừng dùng tổn thương ấy như một vũ khí hiện tại — đối với người kia, hoặc đối với chính mình.

Có một sự phức tạp nữa. Một số tổn thương không nợ cá nhân, mà nợ chính mối quan hệ. Một người bạn đã nói dối bạn nợ bạn; nhưng họ cũng nợ chính tình bạn, vốn đã bị suy yếu. Bạn có thể tha thứ cá nhân cho họ, mà tình bạn vẫn có thể không trở lại hình dạng cũ. Đó không phải là một sự thất bại của tha thứ. Đó là sự thừa nhận rằng có những món nợ có hơn một chủ nợ.

Không có công thức nào cho bất cứ điều nào trong số này. Toán học của sự tha thứ, nếu có thể gọi vậy, là toán học của phép trừ, không phải của phép xóa bỏ. Một thứ gì đó được lấy ra khỏi tài khoản. Một thứ khác, không thể giản lược, vẫn ở lại. Vờ điều ngược lại là hiểu sai mục đích của sự tha thứ. Nó không phải là cách làm cho quá khứ chưa từng xảy ra. Nó là cách để tương lai được đến.

questions.prompt: What asymmetry does the writer describe at the start of the essay?
options: Between the way debts of money and debts of harm can be cancelled
questions.explanation: 'A debt of money can usually be cancelled with a stroke of a pen… A debt of harm, however… cannot be cancelled the same way.'

questions.prompt: What three examples of 'a debt of harm' does the writer give?
options: A loan, a tax, a fine
A betrayal, a cruelty, a broken promise
A theft, a lie, a fight
A mistake, an accident, a surprise
questions.explanation: 'A betrayal, a cruelty, a broken promise.'

questions.prompt: What does the writer say happens 'even when' someone sincerely says, "I forgive you"?
questions.explanation: 'Even when the wronged party says, sincerely, "I forgive you," something stays.'
questions.hint: Look at the end of paragraph 1.

questions.prompt: Why does the writer say money is different from harm?
options: Money is fungible — one note is the same as another — whereas harm is specific to its event
questions.explanation: 'Money is fungible — one note is the same as another — whereas harm is not.'

questions.prompt: What is the writer's definition of forgiveness?
options: Pretending the harm did not happen
A decision to stop charging interest on the debt — the event remains, but it stops growing
questions.explanation: 'Forgiveness, then, is not the cancellation of the debt; it is a decision to stop charging interest on it.'
questions.hint: Look at the end of paragraph 3.

options: Believing that forgiveness means pretending the injury did not occur, or feeling no further pain
questions.explanation: 'People sometimes think that forgiveness means pretending the injury did not occur, or feeling no further pain about it.'
questions.hint: Look at the start of paragraph 4.

questions.prompt: What does forgiveness 'ask only' of us, according to the writer?
options: That we forget the injury completely
That we stop using the injury as a current weapon against the other or against ourselves
That we apologise to the person who harmed us
That we make the other person pay
questions.explanation: 'Forgiveness asks only that you stop using the injury as a current weapon — against the other, or against yourself.'
questions.hint: Look at the end of paragraph 4.

questions.prompt: What further complication does paragraph 5 describe?
options: That some injuries are owed not just to the person but to the relationship itself
That some people forgive too easily
questions.explanation: 'Some injuries are owed not to the person but to the relationship.'

questions.prompt: What does the writer mean by 'more than one creditor'?
options: Both the person and the relationship hold a claim on the debt; forgiving the person does not automatically restore the relationship
The same debt is owed twice
questions.explanation: 'Some debts have more than one creditor.' Personal forgiveness does not necessarily heal the relational debt.
questions.hint: Look at the end of paragraph 5.

questions.prompt: How does the writer describe 'the mathematics of forgiveness'?
questions.explanation: 'The mathematics of forgiveness… is the mathematics of subtraction, not of erasure.'

questions.prompt: What does the writer say forgiveness is NOT?
options: Not a way of unhappening the past
questions.explanation: 'It is not a way of unhappening the past.'

questions.prompt: What does the writer say forgiveness IS for?
questions.explanation: 'It is a way of letting the future arrive.'
questions.hint: Look at the very last sentence.

questions.prompt: Which paraphrase is closer to the writer's view about 'I have failed to forgive'?
options: If you still remember the injury at all, you have failed.
If you have stopped using the injury as a current weapon, you have not failed — even if the memory remains.
Failure is the normal outcome of forgiveness.
You should keep trying to feel no pain at all.
questions.explanation: The writer treats the test 'feel no pain' as the wrong test; remembering is allowed.
questions.hint: Look at paragraph 4's correction of the common error.

questions.prompt: Which of these is the writer NOT claiming?
options: That forgiveness leaves something irreducible in place
That a debt of harm can be compensated but not undone
That forgiveness must always restore the relationship to exactly what it was
That some debts have more than one creditor
questions.explanation: 'You can forgive them personally, and yet the friendship may not return to its old shape. This is not a failure of forgiveness.' The writer explicitly denies this.

questions.prompt: What is the writer's tone toward people who feel they have 'failed' to forgive?
options: Sympathetic — they have applied the wrong test, and the writer is gently re-framing it
questions.explanation: He calls their conclusion 'the wrong test', and offers a corrected one rather than blame.
questions.hint: Look at the language about self-judgement in paragraph 4.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The writer argues that forgiveness is 'a way of letting the future arrive', not a way of unhappening the past. Think of something you have been told to forgive — or that you have wished someone would forgive in you. Which version were you applying: erasure, or subtraction? Did the standard you held yourself to make forgiveness more possible, or less?
